items.title: Thanks to the community report, we recently identified a PR https://t.co/QWboSmskkF that attempted t...
items.content:
Thanks to the community report, we recently identified a PR https://t.co/QWboSmskkF that attempted to solve a non-existent issue and was submitted as part of a “PR training” workflow for resume building.

The contributor involved has been banned from the vLLM community.

This kind of low-signal contribution increases maintainer review overhead and creates unnecessary operational costs for open-source projects.

As AI coding agents make generating large volumes of small PRs increasingly cheap, open-source communities will need to explore new ways to preserve contribution quality and reviewer trust.

While we are investigating how to deal with AI slop, we continue to highly value contributions from real users solving real production problems.

If you have an important contribution that has not yet received maintainer attention, please email us at:

pr-review-request@vllm.ai

Using a verifiable company or university email, include:
- your production or research use case
- the problem you encountered
- how your contribution addresses it

This helps us better prioritize impactful contributions while keeping the vLLM community open and collaborative.

As AI makes virtual contributors look increasingly real, authentic human collaboration matters more than ever.

vLLM’s mission remains unchanged: to make LLM inference easy, fast, and cheap for everyone — and we will continue working toward that goal.

items.content:
People often ask what my biggest tip is for getting the most out of Claude Code.

These days my #1 tip is: use auto mode

Auto mode means no more permission prompts. It is the key building block for multi-clauding: start a session, then while it runs, work on another session in parallel.

⚠️⚠️⚠️i don’t think most people understand the implications of the mood shift below, so I will spell them out. they are serious, and eventually will affect the global economy. 

when a serious coder as hardcore AI-philic as GeoHotz dives deep into all the latest coding models and winds up echoing what I have been saying about the code turning out slop—and how that is going to mess over big companies—the whole genAI movement is a deadman walking.

‘cause coding was the tentpole. and its net impact may actually be net negative—and certainly not big enough to justify the massive investments.

once people figure that out, the bubble will pop.

items.title: Classical AI planners can find optimal solutions to combinatorial problems like sliding-tile puzzles...
items.content:
Classical AI planners can find optimal solutions to combinatorial problems like sliding-tile puzzles in milliseconds, but only if a human first writes the problem out in a symbolic language such as PDDL that names every proposition and every action together with its preconditions and effects.

This requirement has kept symbolic planning largely separate from raw sensor data such as photographs, where there are no pre-existing symbols, only pixels. The authors of this paper build a system called Latplan that learns the symbolic representation on its own from nothing more than unlabeled pairs of before-and-after images of the environment, then hands the learned model to an off-the-shelf planner like Fast Downward. 

The core construction is a discrete autoencoder — a neural network that compresses an input into a smaller representation and reconstructs it, where the compressed representation is forced to be a vector of 0/1 bits rather than real numbers — that maps each image to a binary state vector whose individual bits act as propositions, paired with a second network that learns action effects in a form matching STRIPS rules, meaning each action sets a fixed set of bits to true and another fixed set to false regardless of the rest of the state, so the result can be exported directly as PDDL.

Read with an AI tutor and quizzes for better retention: https://t.co/3QH9CxVLaF

PDF: https://t.co/lsTAgE3LHJ

items.title:
You can now boost any LLM's accuracy 2-10x without training it. 

Most teams improve model accuracy ...
items.content:
You can now boost any LLM's accuracy 2-10x without training it. 

Most teams improve model accuracy by fine-tuning or swapping to a bigger model. 

Both cost time and money.
 
OptiLLM takes a different route. 

It is an open-source proxy that sits between your app and any OpenAI-compatible API.
 
Instead of training, it spends extra compute at inference time to think harder before answering. 

The repo bundles 20+ reasoning techniques you can switch on with one parameter.
 
A few of the methods inside:
 
> Multi-agent cross-verification
> Monte Carlo tree search
> Chain-of-thought with reflection
> Best-of-N sampling
> Z3 theorem prover routing
 
The numbers are the headline. 

On AIME 2025, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite jumps from 43.3% to 73.3% accuracy.
 
Llama 3.3 70B gains 18.6 points on Math-L5. 

GPT-4o-mini matches GPT-4 on Arena-Hard-Auto.
 
No retraining. Just route your calls through the proxy.

items.title:
The call for the NeurIPS 2026 Creative AI Track is out!

In its fourth year, NeurIPS 2026 Creative A...
items.content:
The call for the NeurIPS 2026 Creative AI Track is out!

In its fourth year, NeurIPS 2026 Creative AI Track invites research papers and artworks that explore emerging applications, methods, and critiques of artificial intelligence and machine learning in art, design, and creative practice.

Focusing on the theme of Agency, this year’s track asks: how agency emerges, is exercised, is negotiated, and is contested through creative practice with AI. Agency may belong to an artist, a collaborator, a model, an audience, a platform, a community, or even a larger social and technical system, and may be asserted, delegated, shared, resisted, constrained, or redistributed.

Important dates:
June 30: Submission Portal Opens
August 3 (Anywhere on earth): Submission Deadline 
September 18: Decision 
October 23: Final Camera-Ready Submission

For more information, visit: https://t.co/ju2vjjMKfI

items.title:
GitHub just fixed the biggest problem with vibe coding.
 
Most agents fail the same way. You give a ...
items.content:
GitHub just fixed the biggest problem with vibe coding.
 
Most agents fail the same way. You give a vague prompt and hope they don't break the project.
 
Spec Kit works differently. 

It forces the AI to write a structured specification BEFORE touching any code.
 
The agent reads what you want, asks about missing details, lays out the project, then starts building.
 
You drive it through six slash commands:

1. /constitution sets the rules
2. /specify describes the goal
3. /clarify surfaces open questions
4. /plan picks the stack
5. /tasks lists ordered steps
6. /implement runs the build
 
Every step writes a Markdown file the next one reads.
 
It works with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex, Gemini CLI, and 25 more agents.
 
The open-source repo crossed 95K stars and 8K forks in days.
 
What would you build first with it?

1. /constitution sets the rules

2. /specify describes the goal

3. /clarify surfaces open questions

4. /plan picks the stack

5. /tasks lists ordered steps

6. /implement runs the build

Every step writes a Markdown file the next one reads.

It works with Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex, Gemini CLI, and 25 more agents.

The open-source repo crossed 95K stars and 8K forks in days.

What would you build first with it?

items.title:
Every Sunday lately I join @wholemars’ subscriber-only audio space. 

For the most enthusiastic Tesl...
items.content:
Every Sunday lately I join @wholemars’ subscriber-only audio space. 

For the most enthusiastic Tesla fan. It is quite good. Having to be a subscriber keeps the quality very high. I am thinking of doing the same for my subscribers.

Tomorrow I will lay out why @elonmusk would be smart to sell this for $20,000 each.

Oh hell, why not now?

“You are nuts,” my friend Jeff said after I laid this out on a long ride as my robot drove us to Walnut Creek and back for spicy chicken.

But I laid out why I am not. Elon’s goal should be to get seven million on street as fast as possible.

Selling them for less than expected will do a few things.

1. He will make a ton of money on each after the sale. Mine will never sit in my garage. Always will be driving people around. Customers, we call them.

2. It guarantees he will sell fast. If it were that cheap I would buy more than one.

3. It would cause hyper evangelistic behavior. The Tesla community is solid here. If it were that cheap everyone would talk about it.

Now would @elonmusk ? He knows I would pay $40,000 to own one. I paid him $75,000 for far worse (which I really love, it is an amazing car).

But my argument to him is he will better fulfill his original dream by pricing hyper aggressively.

When he gave me the first ride in the first Tesla he told me he would bring electric’s advantages to the world: primarily that electric vehicles would be a lot more affordable for everyone.

See walk down my street. Every vehicle sitting in driveways is an unused asset.

If we all shared vehicles they would get dramatically cheaper.

And Elon knows that Uber went from an idea in a snowstorm in Paris (I was there talking to Travis and Garrett when they were thinking through their idea too) to driving housewives in South African slums in four years (I visited the slums too).

If he is aggressive on price he will get there faster.

The razor / blade argument. Give away the razor and charge for the blades.

The Cybercab is the razor. Robotaxi is the blades.

I assume he will take 2/3rds of the dollars collected by Robotaxi.

Each one of these will be a money printing machine.

Why do you think @woodhaus2 over indexed on the gold? 

:-)

Even better that the Chinese love gold so much. They are crazy about Gold.

But how Elon prices the Cybercab will tell us volumes about how aggressive he will be about competing.

I will buy one either way and put it into the Robotaxi network. 

Will report how good the checks are it seems me every month.

It is the future of transportation.

items.title: This kinda ties into the "you're competing with non-tech people now" from the tweet yday about the I...
items.content:
This kinda ties into the "you're competing with non-tech people now" from the tweet yday about the Indonesian girl getting to $800 MRR within a month

It's all about your idea plugged into the cultural zeitgeist and then your style of execution which is based on who you are a s person

Every little thing you experienced influences the choices you make when building a product too, small tiny details that you do different that are unmeasurable but turn out to be a big reason why users like your product over others

For me the easiest way to get more life experience always has been to just go travel, even better travel for loooong times, live in foreign places by yourself for months (maybe years), preferrably solo, something happens to you that changes you as a person

You wanna do this in your 20s/30s but you can do it any age, it's just that if you're not single anymore, your style of travel usually changes into more normie patterns but you can still do it

Go to places where few other people go, I always talk about China because so few people visit it, yet it's a world leader now in so many things, you'll learn so many things just being there

For me it started when I studied abroad in 2009 in Korea, it reset my mind and identity is such a fundamental way that everything that came after for me (like going nomad in 2013, building startups, becoming a perpetual immigrant away from my home country forever) can kinda be lead to that moment

Fly somewhere far for months, by yourself, if you can, and you'll get those life experiences that will change you forever, make you a better person and also help you make better products!

items.title: @BillSabo11 @wholemars @elonmusk Well, I agree eventually. But up front if you have one you will be ...
items.content:
@BillSabo11 @wholemars @elonmusk Well, I agree eventually. But up front if you have one you will be in demand in San Francisco. 

But it all depends on the deal. Which we haven't heard yet. 

So this is all a little bit of dreaming at the moment. 

My current car has 162,000 miles on it. It might last until 300,000. But I sold my Prius to buy my Tesla after 110,000 miles. And if I get stuck with this as my next car I'd be plenty fine with that. But I expect I'll put it into the Robotaxi network first to make money until my car dies. 

We'll see. 

Either way, Tesla needs to put millions of these on the road and they've only made 100 or so so far.

We'll see.

Either way, Tesla needs to put millions of these on the road and they've only made 100 or so so far.

In head on wrecks it is the steering wheel that kills you.

items.content:
Tarantino: "You have to retire at 60 to avoid making crap."

Nolan at 55: "Hold my beer."

===

Nolan's best films were Memento and The Dark Knight Trilogy. Then it kept getting worse. The absolutely dumb Tenet and banal, boring Oppenheimer were already time wasters.

Now The Odyssey, based on a woke, talentless translation of the original poem, with a ridiculous The Rings of Power-style miscast and 3D-printed, modern-looking armor.

Christopher, thanks for Memento and The Dark Knight, but your retirement age should have been 50.

items.content:
The Boroughs on Netflix is done very well.

It's evident that the team is the same as the one behind Stranger Things. The picture is great. The cast is great. 

But the plot is weak so the series is just good enough for a single watch. And you have to be very patient because, in the first three episodes, nothing happens.

I hope they don't renew it for a second season.
